title: Broken Object Level Authorization — resource accessed without ownership check
description: API endpoint retrieves or modifies a resource by ID from request parameters without verifying that the authenticated user owns or has permission to access it. This is the #1 API vulnerability (BOLA/IDOR).

remediation.description: Always verify resource ownership after fetching. Add a WHERE clause that includes the authenticated user's ID, or check ownership before returning the resource.

title: Missing authentication on sensitive API endpoint
description: API endpoint handling sensitive data or actions does not require authentication, allowing unauthenticated access.

remediation.description: Apply authentication middleware to all sensitive endpoints. Use a default-deny approach: require authentication everywhere, then explicitly mark public routes.

title: JWT signature not properly verified
description: JWT token accepted without proper signature verification, or using algorithm confusion (e.g., accepting 'none' algorithm).

remediation.description: Always use jwt.verify() with explicit algorithm specification. Never accept the 'none' algorithm. Validate issuer and audience claims.

title: Mass assignment vulnerability — unfiltered request body
description: API endpoint directly passes the request body to create/update operations without filtering, allowing attackers to set privileged fields (e.g., role, isAdmin, price).

remediation.description: Explicitly pick allowed fields from the request body. Never pass the entire request body to create/update operations.

title: Excessive data exposure in API response
description: API endpoint returns full database objects including sensitive fields (passwords, tokens, internal IDs) that the client doesn't need.

remediation.description: Use DTOs or select specific fields in queries. Never return raw database objects. Strip sensitive fields before responding.

title: Missing rate limiting on API endpoint
description: API endpoint has no rate limiting, allowing unlimited requests that can lead to DoS, brute-force attacks, or excessive resource consumption.

remediation.description: Apply rate limiting to all API endpoints. Use different limits for different endpoint types (auth: strict, read: moderate, write: moderate).

title: Missing pagination limit on list endpoint
description: API list endpoint returns unbounded results or accepts arbitrarily large page sizes, enabling resource exhaustion attacks.

remediation.description: Always enforce maximum page size limits. Set sensible defaults and cap user-requested page sizes.

title: GraphQL query without depth/complexity limits
description: GraphQL API accepts queries without depth or complexity limits, allowing deeply nested queries that can cause exponential resource consumption.

remediation.description: Add query depth limiting and complexity analysis. Limit maximum query depth to 5-10 levels and set a complexity budget per query.

title: Admin endpoint without role verification
description: Administrative API endpoint lacks role-based access control, potentially allowing regular users to access admin functionality.

remediation.description: Implement role-based access control (RBAC) middleware. Apply admin role checks to all administrative endpoints.

title: API endpoint fetches user-supplied URL
description: API endpoint makes server-side HTTP requests to a URL provided in the request, enabling SSRF attacks against internal services or cloud metadata endpoints.

remediation.description: Validate URLs against an allowlist of domains. Block private/internal IP ranges. Use DNS resolution checks to prevent DNS rebinding.

title: Verbose error responses exposing stack traces
description: API returns detailed error messages including stack traces, database queries, or internal paths in production, providing valuable information to attackers.

remediation.description: Return generic error messages in production. Log detailed errors server-side but never expose them to clients.

title: API allows unnecessary HTTP methods
description: API endpoint responds to HTTP methods it shouldn't (e.g., GET endpoint also accepts DELETE), increasing attack surface.

remediation.description: Use specific HTTP method handlers instead of app.all(). Return 405 Method Not Allowed for unsupported methods.

title: Deprecated API version still accessible
description: Old API versions remain accessible without deprecation headers or shutdown dates, increasing maintenance burden and attack surface.

remediation.description: Set deprecation headers and sunset dates for old API versions. Redirect or return 410 Gone for fully deprecated endpoints.

title: Third-party API response used without validation
description: Data received from external/third-party APIs used directly without validation or sanitization, creating injection vectors via untrusted external data.

remediation.description: Validate all data received from third-party APIs against expected schemas. Treat external API data as untrusted input.
